---
title: "Propositional Logic: Operators, Truth Tables and Syllogisms"
description: "Formalise statements using the basic logical operators (not, and, or, if-then), build truth tables to work out when compound statements are true, and recognise the common valid syllogisms — and the fallacies that imitate them."
author:
  - "Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch"
  - "Anna-Katharina Kothe"
date: 2026-06-17
date-modified: 2026-06-17
image: ../../assets/images/placeholder.png

learning-objectives:
  - "Translate plain-language statements into symbols using the operators ¬, ∧, ∨ and →"
  - "Construct truth tables for negation, conjunction, disjunction and the conditional"
  - "Explain why a contradiction is always false and a tautology always true"
  - "Recognise modus ponens, modus tollens, hypothetical and disjunctive syllogisms, and distinguish them from the fallacies of the inverse and the converse"

categories:
  - philosophy
  - logic
  - argumentation
  - BA
  - concept

level:
  - BA
  - MA

content-type: concept

citation:
  type: entry
  container-title: "Methods Hub"
  title: "Propositional Logic: Operators, Truth Tables and Syllogisms"
  author:
    - name: "Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch"
    - name: "Anna-Katharina Kothe"
  editor:
    - name: "Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch"
  url: https://euf-methodology-hub.netlify.app/resources/philosophy/propositional-logic-operators-truth-tables-syllogisms
---

# Introduction

Once you can reconstruct an argument into premises and a conclusion (see [Constructing and Reconstructing Arguments](constructing-and-reconstructing-arguments.qmd)), the next step is to analyse its *form*. **Propositional logic** is the study of how the meaning of logical operators — words like *not*, *and*, *or*, and *if … then* — determines the truth conditions of compound statements, and which patterns of inference are reliable.

The payoff is practical: a small set of symbols and tables lets you see at a glance whether a piece of reasoning is built on a valid pattern or a tempting imitation of one.

# Notation: Writing Logic

A few conventions make reasoning easier to handle:

- Use **symbols** (letters such as p, q, s, …) to stand for whole statements ("propositions").
- Separate the conclusion from the premises with a line, or with the symbol **∴** ("therefore").
- Use **logical operators** to combine statements.
- Assign **truth values** (T, F) to the component statements and work out the truth value of the whole — this is what a **truth table** does.

For example, the argument

> Premise 1: If it rains, the street is wet.
> Premise 2: It rains.
> Conclusion: The street is wet.

becomes, with `r` = "it rains" and `w` = "the street is wet":

> P1: r → w
> P2: r
> ∴ w

# The Basic Logical Operators

## Negation (¬)

`¬p` ("not p") reverses the truth value of `p`.

> p ⇒ "you shall pass"
> ¬p ⇒ "you shall not pass"

For `¬p` to be true, `p` must be false; for `¬p` to be false, `p` must be true.

## Conjunction (∧)

`p ∧ q` ("p and q") is true only when **both** `p` and `q` are true. Order does not matter: `p ∧ q ≡ q ∧ p`.

## Disjunction (∨)

`p ∨ q` ("p or q") is true when **at least one** of `p`, `q` is true. (This is the *inclusive* or — it is also true when both hold.) Order does not matter: `p ∨ q ≡ q ∨ p`.

## The conditional (→)

`p → q` ("if p, then q") links a condition `p` to a consequence `q`. Unlike the others, **order matters**: `p → q` is not the same as `q → p`.

The conditional has one feature that surprises newcomers:

- If `p` is true, then `p → q` takes the truth value of `q`.
- If `p` is **false**, then `p → q` is **true by definition** (the promise was never triggered, so it cannot have been broken).

# Truth Tables

A truth table lists every combination of truth values for the components and gives the resulting value of the compound statement.

**Negation**

| p | ¬p |
|---|----|
| T | F  |
| F | T  |

**Conjunction and disjunction**

| p | q | p ∧ q | p ∨ q |
|---|---|-------|-------|
| T | T | T | T |
| T | F | F | T |
| F | T | F | T |
| F | F | F | F |

**The conditional**

| p | q | p → q |
|---|---|-------|
| T | T | T |
| T | F | F |
| F | T | T |
| F | F | T |

The only way to make `p → q` false is to have a true condition `p` with a false consequence `q` — the second row.

# Logical Truths: Contradictions and Tautologies

Two compound statements are worth memorising because their truth value never depends on the facts:

- `p ∧ ¬p` is a **contradiction** — always false. (For a conjunction to be true we need both parts true, but `p` and `¬p` can never both be true.) *Example: "g is green and g is not green."*
- `p ∨ ¬p` is a **tautology** — always true. (For a disjunction we need at least one part true, and exactly one of `p`, `¬p` always is.) *Example: "the door is open or it is not open."*

# Common Syllogisms

A **syllogism** is a valid deductive argument with (typically) two premises and a conclusion. If the premises are true and the syllogism is valid, the conclusion *must* be true. Four patterns recur constantly.

**Modus ponens** — affirm the condition:

> P → Q
> P
> ∴ Q
>
> *If it rains, the street is wet. It rains. ∴ The street is wet.*

**Modus tollens** — deny the consequence:

> P → Q
> ¬Q
> ∴ ¬P
>
> *If our pricing strategy is competitive, sales will increase. Sales have not increased. ∴ Our pricing strategy is not competitive.*

**Hypothetical syllogism** — chain two conditionals:

> P → Q
> Q → R
> ∴ P → R
>
> *If I don't wake up, I can't go to work. If I can't go to work, I won't get paid. ∴ If I don't wake up, I won't get paid.*

**Disjunctive syllogism** — eliminate one option:

> P ∨ Q
> ¬P
> ∴ Q
>
> *I eat soup today or I eat salad today. I do not eat soup today. ∴ I eat salad today.*

# Two Tempting Fallacies

Two invalid patterns *look* like modus ponens and modus tollens but are not valid. Watch for them.

| Valid | | Invalid |
|---|---|---|
| **Modus ponens** | | **Fallacy of the converse** |
| P → Q; P; ∴ Q | | P → Q; **Q**; ∴ **P** |
| **Modus tollens** | | **Fallacy of the inverse** |
| P → Q; ¬Q; ∴ ¬P | | P → Q; **¬P**; ∴ **¬Q** |

Using "If it rains, the street is wet":

- **Fallacy of the converse:** "The street is wet, ∴ it rains." Invalid — the street could be wet for another reason.
- **Fallacy of the inverse:** "It is not raining, ∴ the street is not wet." Invalid for the same reason.

::: {.callout-note .level-advanced}
## 🎓 MA / PhD

Propositional logic is the floor, not the ceiling. It treats statements as indivisible atoms; **predicate (first-order) logic** adds quantifiers ("for all", "there exists") and lets you reason about relationships *within* statements, which is needed for most genuinely scientific generalisations. The valid argument forms above also reappear as *rules of inference* in formal proof systems. If you work with formal models, the connection between truth tables and **logical validity** (a conclusion is valid iff there is no row in which all premises are true and the conclusion false) is worth internalising.
:::

# Summary

- The operators **¬, ∧, ∨, →** combine statements; their **truth tables** define exactly when the compound is true.
- `p ∧ ¬p` is always false (**contradiction**); `p ∨ ¬p` is always true (**tautology**).
- **Modus ponens, modus tollens, hypothetical** and **disjunctive** syllogisms are valid patterns.
- The **fallacy of the converse** and the **fallacy of the inverse** imitate the valid conditional patterns but are invalid.

# What to Read Next

- [Evaluating Arguments: Validity, Soundness and Fallacies](evaluating-arguments-validity-soundness-fallacies.qmd) — moving from form to a full evaluation of whether an argument is *good*.

# Further Reading

- Gensler, H. J. (2010). *Introduction to Logic* (2nd ed.). Routledge.
- Hurley, P. J. (2000). *A Concise Introduction to Logic* (7th ed.). Wadsworth.
